title: Merck — Vaccines — briefing Q1-2026
html:
Merck Vaccines Quarterly Briefing — Q1 2026

Quarter: January–March 2026 Earnings reported: April 2026 (expected)

Governing Thought

Merck's vaccine division enters 2026 with zero China GARDASIL revenue baked into guidance, CDC single-dose HPV guidance on the horizon, and V940 Phase 3 trials actively enrolling across melanoma and NSCLC. The question has shifted from "will GARDASIL recover?" to "what replaces GARDASIL as the vaccine growth engine?"

1. Financial Signals

Metric Q1 2026 (est.) Commentary
GARDASIL (est.) ~$1.0-1.3B US-only baseline; $0 China per guidance
VAXELIS Modest growth Steady pediatric adoption
Merck Q1 2026 Awaiting full results Focus on Keytruda LOE defense and pipeline
  • GARDASIL is now a $4-5B US/ex-China asset — smaller but stable
  • CDC single-dose guidance implementation timing will determine the next volume trajectory
  • Merck's strategic communication is shifting emphasis from vaccine revenue to pipeline optionality (V940)

What Merck management has guided (pre-earnings — Q1 2026 reports April 30):

  1. FY2026 guidance assumes $0 China GARDASIL revenue. The US/ex-China franchise (~$4-5B) is the entire GARDASIL base for 2026. Consensus for Q1 2026 GARDASIL is ~$1.0-1.3B (seasonal, US-only baseline).
  2. Management's strategic communication has shifted from "GARDASIL recovery" to "pipeline transformation." At JPM 2026 and subsequent conferences, V940 (individualized cancer vaccine with Moderna) was positioned as the next-generation vaccine growth engine, with GARDASIL explicitly reframed as a "stable base" rather than a growth asset.
  3. The CDC single-dose HPV guidance has not yet been formally implemented. Management has acknowledged the risk (~60% of current US GARDASIL doses could be affected) but has not provided specific revenue impact estimates — suggesting internal modeling is still in progress.
  4. Adult HPV catch-up vaccination (27-45 age group) was mentioned at investor conferences as a potential growth vector to offset adolescent volume pressure from single-dose guidance. No promotional investment details have been disclosed.

Key analyst Q&A signals (from Q4 2025 call + conference commentary):

  1. Vaccine questions have shifted from "when will China recover" to "what replaces GARDASIL as the vaccine growth engine." This narrative shift from recovery to replacement signals that the Street has written off China GARDASIL as a lost franchise.
  2. Analysts are beginning to model CDC single-dose HPV impact into their US GARDASIL forecasts. The consensus is that single-dose implementation would reduce US GARDASIL revenue by $1.5-2.5B — a material headwind even without China.
  3. V940's 2027 Phase 3 readout is too distant for analysts to incorporate into near-term models. The vaccine division faces a 2-3 year gap where GARDASIL declines are not offset by pipeline revenue — a challenging period for Merck's vaccine economics.

2. Competitive Moves

  • V940 clinical momentum: Three Phase 3 trials enrolling (INTerpath-001 fully enrolled in melanoma, INTerpath-002 and -009 in NSCLC). The January 2026 5-year data presentation (49% recurrence reduction) generated significant KOL interest.
  • Moderna partnership: V940 manufacturing leverages Moderna's mRNA production infrastructure — a scaling advantage if Phase 3 succeeds
  • Adult HPV vaccination: Potential growth vector if Merck promotes catch-up vaccination (27-45 age group) to offset adolescent volume decline
  • PNEUMOVAX: Mature, declining product — not a growth driver

3. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q1 2026

Company Brand Q1 2026 Revenue (est.) Key Signal
GSK Cervarix Immaterial GSK acknowledged Gardasil dominance in HPV
Pfizer Prevnar 20 ~$1.5B Prevnar 20 maintains dominance; Vaxneuvance struggling (~$160M)
GARDASIL ~$1.0-1.3B US-only baseline; $0 China per guidance

GSK — Cervarix: GSK's Q1 2026 vaccine commentary focused on Arexvy (RSV) and Shingrix, with Cervarix revenue immaterial in most markets. GSK has effectively conceded the HPV vaccine market to Gardasil. There is no indication of renewed investment, label expansion, or promotional push for Cervarix. GARDASIL's 95%+ global HPV market share is unchallenged.

Pfizer — Prevnar 20 (~$1.5B): Prevnar 20 maintains pneumococcal dominance at $1.5B quarterly. Merck's Vaxneuvance ($160M) continues to struggle in the PCV15 vs. PCV20 battle. The pneumococcal competitive loss is now structural — Pfizer's broader serotype coverage and earlier market entry have created switching inertia that Vaxneuvance cannot overcome. This competitive failure in pneumococcal makes GARDASIL's revenue stability even more critical to Merck's vaccine division economics.

Synthesis: GARDASIL's competitive position remains unique in pharma: dominant market share with no branded challenger, but declining revenue driven entirely by market dynamics. The China domestic 9-valent competitor, zero-China guidance for 2026, and potential CDC single-dose HPV guidance are the threats — not GSK or any other vaccine company. Merck's vaccine future depends on stabilizing the US GARDASIL franchise (~$4-5B) and successfully developing V940 as the next-generation growth engine. The Vaxneuvance loss to Prevnar 20 makes this GARDASIL stabilization non-negotiable.

4. Clinical/Regulatory

  • V940 regulatory path: INTerpath-001 melanoma readout expected 2027 — the pivotal data point for the entire franchise
  • INTerpath-009 NSCLC: Novel design targeting patients without pathological complete response after neoadjuvant Keytruda + chemo — addresses a specific unmet need
  • V940 manufacturing: Individualized neoantigen sequencing + mRNA production creates unprecedented supply chain requirements — commercial feasibility is the unknown variable
  • CDC single-dose HPV: if implemented, affects ~60% of current GARDASIL doses in the US

5. What It Means for ZoomRx

The Merck vaccine story has bifurcated: maintain GARDASIL base + bet on V940 future.

  1. V940 pre-launch awareness study should be scoped and pitched to Merck in 2026. Oncologist awareness of individualized cancer vaccines, willingness to adopt complex manufacturing logistics, and perceived barriers are all researchable now.

  2. GARDASIL US franchise tracking should focus on CDC single-dose impact — physician behavior change, patient completion rates, and revenue-per-vaccinated-person dynamics.

  3. VAXELIS PET W5 should proceed as planned — this is ZoomRx's most durable vaccine engagement and provides continuity during the GARDASIL transition.

  4. Cross-Keytruda opportunity: V940 is co-administered with Keytruda. ZoomRx already tracks Keytruda in oncology. Positioning V940 tracking as an extension of existing oncology capabilities creates a natural BD path.

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home
